corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: It also outperforms previously proposed dense retrievers which were trained with ICT or salient span masking.
Contriever also outperforms previously proposed unsupervised dense retrievers, which obtains lower performance than BM25 in general.
The difference is mainly due to the fact that BM25 largely outperforms Contriever on two datasets with specific features: Trec-COVID and Tóuche-2020.

We observe that when used as pre-training, contrastive learning leads to strong performance: contriever obtains the best results among dense bi-encoder methods for the nDCG@10, and is state-of-the-art for the recall@100 (improving the average recall@100 from 65.0 to 67.1).

This strong recall@100 performance can be further exploited by using a cross-encoder to re-rank the retrieved documents: this leads to the state-of-the-art on 8 datasets of the BEIR benchmark for the nDCG@10, as well as on average.
Moreover our unsupervised mContriever outperforms BM25 for the Recall@100, and after fine-tuning on MS MARCO it achieves state-of-the-art performance for this metric.
Moreover our unsupervised mContriever outperforms BM25 for the Recall@100, and after fine-tuning on MS MARCO it achieves state-of-the-art performance for this metric.
Moreover our unsupervised mContriever outperforms BM25 for the Recall@100, and after fine-tuning on MS MARCO it achieves state-of-the-art performance for this metric.
Interestingly using only supervised training data in English, our mContriever fine-tuned on MS MARCO outperforms the CORA retriever.
We review the model architecture and then describe contrastive learning -a key component of its training.
Instead, we follow standard approaches in information retrieval and use a bi-encoder architecture where documents and queries are encoded independently.
Following previous work on dense retrieval with neural networks, we use the BERT base uncased architecture and refer the reader to for more details.
We report the top-k retrieval accuracy, i.e. the number of questions for which at least one of the top-k passages contain the answer.

Second, we use the BEIR benchmark, introduced by , which contains 18 retrieval datasets, corresponding to nine tasks, such as fact checking or citation prediction, and covering different domains, such as Wikipedia or scientific publications.
Most datasets from BEIR do not contain a training set, and the focus of the benchmark is zero-shot retrieval.
However, most machine learning based retrievers are still trained on supervised data, such as the large scale retrieval dataset MS MARCO .
Following standard practice, we report two metrics on this benchmark: nDCG@10 and Recall@100.
Following standard practice, we report two metrics on this benchmark: nDCG@10 and Recall@100.
On the other hand, Recall@100 is relevant to evaluate retrievers that are used in machine learning systems, such as question answering.
While nDCG@10 is the main metric of BEIR, we are more interested in the Recall@100 to evaluate bi-encoders, as our goal is to develop retrievers that can be used in ML systems.
Here, our model is competitive with a strong BM25 baseline , for example leading to 3 points improvement for the recall@100 on NaturalQuestions.
In Figure we report the recall@100 performance of unsupervised models on the BEIR benchmark.
In Figure we report the recall@100 performance of unsupervised models on the BEIR benchmark.
In particular, it obtains better performance than BM25 on 11 out of 15 datasets from the benchmark for the recall@100.
In particular, it obtains better performance than BM25 on 11 out of 15 datasets from the benchmark for the recall@100.
